questions.question: For election to the Lok Sabha, a nomination paper can be filed by
questions.option_2: a resident of the constituency from which the election is to be contested.
questions.option_3: any citizen of India whose name appears in the electoral roll of a constituency.
questions.solution:
Nomination for Lok Sabha Elections

The process of filing nomination papers for the Lok Sabha is governed by the Representation of the People Act, 1951.

  • Eligibility: Any citizen of India can file a nomination for a Lok Sabha seat provided they are at least 25 years old.
  • Constituency Requirement: For the Lok Sabha, a candidate does not need to be a resident or a registered voter in the specific constituency they are contesting from. However, their name must appear in the electoral roll of any parliamentary constituency in the country.
  • Exceptions: For seats reserved for Scheduled Castes or Scheduled Tribes, the person must be a member of those categories in any state and a registered voter.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. In the election for Lok Sabha or State Assembly, the winning candidate must get at least 50 percent of the votes polled, to be declared elected.
  2. According to the provisions laid down in the Constitution of India in Lok Sabha, the Speaker's post goes to the majority party and the Deputy Speaker's to the Opposition.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Election Systems and Parliamentary Conventions

This question explores the voting system used in India and the conventions regarding presiding officers.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: India follows the First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) system for Lok Sabha and State Assembly elections. In this system, the candidate who secures the highest number of votes is declared elected, even if they do not reach the 50% mark.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect: The Constitution does not mandate that the Speaker must be from the majority party and the Deputy Speaker from the Opposition. While Article 93 provides for their election, the specific distribution of these posts is a parliamentary convention, not a constitutional provision.

questions.question: The Parliament of India acquires the power to legislate on any item in the State List in the national interest if a resolution to that effect is passed by the
questions.option_1: Lok Sabha by a simple majority of its total membership
questions.option_2: Lok Sabha by a majority of not less than two-thirds of its total membership
questions.option_3: Rajya Sabha by a simple majority of its total membership
questions.option_4: Rajya Sabha by a majority of not less than two-thirds of its members present and voting
questions.solution:
Parliament's Power over State List (Article 249)

Under the federal structure, the State List is normally exclusive to State Legislatures, but the Rajya Sabha can authorize Parliament to intervene.

  • Article 249: If the Rajya Sabha declares by a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest, Parliament can make laws for the whole or any part of India with respect to any matter in the State List.
  • Such a resolution remains in force for a period not exceeding one year, but it can be renewed any number of times.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?

  1. A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses on its prorogation.
  2. A Bill pending in the Rajya Sabha, which has not been passed by the Lok Sabha, shall not lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.

Select the correct answer using the code below:
questions.solution:
Lapsing and Prorogation of Bills

The status of a bill depends on whether the House is merely ending a session or being dissolved entirely.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: Prorogation (ending a session) does not affect the bills or any other business pending before the House. All pending notices (other than for introducing bills) lapse, but the bills themselves do not.
  • Statement 2 is correct: A bill that is pending in the Rajya Sabha but has not yet been passed by the Lok Sabha does not lapse upon the dissolution of the Lok Sabha. This is because the Rajya Sabha is a permanent body.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Legislative Council of a state in India can be larger in size than half of the Legislative Assembly of that particular state.
  2. The Governor of a state nominates the Chairman of the Legislative Council of that particular state.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Composition of State Legislative Councils (Vidhan Parishad)

The creation and composition of the Legislative Council are governed by Article 171.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: According to Article 171, the total number of members in the Legislative Council of a State shall not exceed one-third (not half) of the total number of members in the Legislative Assembly of that State. The minimum strength is fixed at 40.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect: The Chairman of the Legislative Council is elected by the Council from amongst its members. The Governor does not nominate the Chairman; the Governor only nominates 1/6th of the members of the Council.

questions.question: When a bill is referred to a joint sitting of both the Houses of the Parliament, it has to be passed by
questions.option_1: a simple majority of members present and voting
questions.option_2: three-fourths majority of members present and voting
questions.solution:
Joint Sitting of Parliament (Article 108)

A joint sitting is a mechanism to resolve a deadlock between the two Houses over the passage of a bill.

  • Procedure: At a joint sitting, the bill is passed by a simple majority of the total number of members of both Houses present and voting.
  • Since the Lok Sabha has a much larger membership (543) compared to the Rajya Sabha (245), the Lok Sabha generally has the upper hand in a joint sitting.

questions.question: Which one of the following is the largest Committee of the Parliament?
questions.solution:
Financial Committees of Parliament

The Parliament has three main financial committees to ensure executive accountability regarding public funds.

CommitteeTotal MembersComposition
Estimates Committee30All 30 from Lok Sabha only.
Public Accounts Committee (PAC)2215 (LS) + 7 (RS).
Committee on Public Undertakings (CoPU)2215 (LS) + 7 (RS).

The Estimates Committee is the largest committee of Parliament because all its 30 members are drawn from the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: What will follow if a Money Bill is substantially amended by the Rajya Sabha?
questions.option_1: The Lok Sabha may still proceed with the Bill, accepting or not accepting the recommendations of the Rajya Sabha
questions.option_2: The Lok Sabha cannot consider the Bill further
questions.option_3: The Lok Sabha may send the Bill to the Rajya Sabha for reconsideration
questions.option_4: The President may call a joint sitting for passing the Bill
questions.solution:
Rajya Sabha's Power over Money Bills

The Rajya Sabha has extremely limited powers regarding Money Bills (Article 110).

  • When a Money Bill is sent to the Rajya Sabha, it must return the bill within 14 days.
  • The Rajya Sabha can suggest amendments (recommendations), but it cannot actually amend the bill.
  • The Lok Sabha has the absolute power to accept or reject all or any of the recommendations. If the Lok Sabha accepts none, the bill is deemed passed by both Houses in its original form.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Chairman and the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha are not the members of that House.
  2. While the nominated members of the two Houses of the Parliament have no voting right in the presidential election, they have the right to vote in the election of the Vice President.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Officers and Members of Rajya Sabha

The Rajya Sabha is presided over by the Chairman and Deputy Chairman, but their membership status differs.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: The Chairman of the Rajya Sabha (the Vice-President of India) is not a member of the House. However, the Deputy Chairman is elected from among the members of the Rajya Sabha, so they are a member.
  • Statement 2 is correct: Nominated members (12 in RS) cannot vote in the election of the President (who is elected by an electoral college of elected members). However, they do participate in the election of the Vice-President, which consists of all members of both Houses.

questions.question: Which of the following special powers have been conferred on the Rajya Sabha by the Constitution of India?
questions.option_1: To change the existing territory of a State and to change the name of a State
questions.option_2: To pass a resolution empowering the Parliament to make laws in the State List and to create one or more All India Services
questions.option_3: To amend the election procedure of the President and to determine the pension of the President after his/her retirement
questions.option_4: To determine the functions of the Election Commission and to determine the number of Election Commissioners
questions.solution:
Special Powers of Rajya Sabha

As the representative of the States, the Rajya Sabha enjoys certain exclusive powers that the Lok Sabha does not have.

  • Article 249: Power to authorize Parliament to legislate on a State subject.
  • Article 312: Power to authorize the creation of new All India Services (common to both Union and States).
  • Changing state territories (Article 3) or Election Commission functions are powers shared equally with the Lok Sabha or involve the whole Parliament.

questions.question:
Which of the following are the methods of Parliamentary control over public finance in India?

  1. Placing Annual Financial Statement before the Parliament
  2. Withdrawal of money from Consolidated Fund of India only after passing the Appropriation Bill
  3. Provisions of supplementary grants and vote-on-account
  4. Aperiodic or at least a mid-year review of programme of the Government against macroeconomic forecasts and expenditure by a Parliamentary Budget Office
  5. Introducing Finance Bill in the Parliament

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.solution:
Parliamentary Control over Public Finance

The Parliament ensures that not a single paisa is spent by the government without its approval through several stages.

  1. Annual Financial Statement: Laying the budget (Article 112).
  2. Appropriation Bill: Withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund of India is only possible after this bill is passed (Article 114).
  3. Grants: Supplementary, additional, or excess grants and Vote-on-Account (Article 116) allow for temporary or extra funding.
  4. Finance Bill: Necessary for the legal authority to collect taxes.

Note: A 'Parliamentary Budget Office' is not a formal constitutional or legal requirement for mid-year reviews in India currently, though it exists in other democracies.

questions.question: In the Parliament of India, the purpose of an adjournment motion is:
questions.option_1: To allow a discussion on a definite matter of urgent public importance
questions.option_2: To let opposition collect information from the ministers
questions.option_3: To allow a reduction of specific amount in demand for grant
questions.option_4: To postpone the proceedings to check the inappropriate or violent behavior on the part of some members
questions.solution:
Purpose of an Adjournment Motion

An adjournment motion is an extraordinary device used in the Lok Sabha to draw the attention of the House to a definite matter of urgent public importance.

  • It interrupts the regular business of the House.
  • Since it involves an element of censure against the government, it requires the support of at least 50 members to be admitted.
  • Rajya Sabha is not permitted to use this device.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. Union Territories are not represented in the Rajya Sabha.
  2. It is within the preview of the Chief Election Commissioner to adjudicate election disputes.
  3. According to the Constitution of India, the Parliament consists of the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha only.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Representation and Structure of Parliament

This question clarifies common misconceptions about the composition and legal oversight of Parliament.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: Some Union Territories (Delhi, Puducherry, and Jammu & Kashmir) are represented in the Rajya Sabha.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect: Post the 19th Amendment (1966), election disputes are tried by High Courts, with the right of appeal to the Supreme Court. The CEC does not adjudicate these disputes.
  • Statement 3 is incorrect: According to Article 79, the Parliament consists of the President, the Lok Sabha, and the Rajya Sabha. The President is an integral part of the Parliament.

questions.question:
Regarding the office of the Lok Sabha Speaker, consider the following statements:

  1. He/She holds the office during the pleasure of the President.
  2. He/She need not be a member of the House at the time of his/her election but has to become a member of the House within six months from the date of his/her election.
  3. If he/she intends to resign, the letter of his/her resignation has to be addressed to the Deputy Speaker.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The Office of the Lok Sabha Speaker

The Speaker is the head of the Lok Sabha and its representative.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: The Speaker does not hold office during the 'pleasure of the President'. They are elected by the House and can be removed only by a resolution passed by a majority of all then members of the Lok Sabha.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect: A person must be a member of the Lok Sabha at the time of their election as Speaker.
  • Statement 3 is correct: Under Article 94, the Speaker submits their resignation in writing to the Deputy Speaker (and vice versa).

questions.question: The authorization for the withdrawal of funds from the Consolidated Fund of India must come from
questions.solution:
Consolidated Fund of India (Article 266)

The Consolidated Fund is the primary account of the Government of India.

  • No money can be withdrawn from this fund except under appropriation made by law passed by the Parliament (Appropriation Act).
  • The President, PM, or Finance Minister cannot authorize withdrawals independently of the parliamentary process.

questions.question: All revenues received by the Union Government by way of taxes and other receipts for the conduct of Government business are credited to the
questions.solution:
Revenues and the Consolidated Fund

Under Article 266(1), all revenues received by the Government of India, all loans raised by that Government, and all moneys received by it in repayment of loans shall form one consolidated fund entitled 'the Consolidated Fund of India'.

  • Public Account: Holds other public moneys like provident fund deposits and judicial deposits.
  • Contingency Fund: For meeting unforeseen expenditure pending authorization by Parliament.

questions.question: When the annual Union Budget is not passed by the Lok Sabha,
questions.option_1: the Budget is modified and presented again
questions.option_2: the Budget is referred to the Rajya Sabha for suggestions
questions.option_3: the Union Finance Minister is asked to resign
questions.option_4: the Prime Minister submits the resignation of Council of Ministers
questions.solution:
Failure to Pass the Union Budget

The passage of the Budget is essentially a test of the government's majority in the Lok Sabha.

  • If the Annual Financial Statement (Budget) or the Appropriation Bill is defeated in the Lok Sabha, it signifies that the government has lost the confidence of the House.
  • In such a case, the Prime Minister must submit the resignation of the entire Council of Ministers.

questions.question:
What is the difference between "vote-on-account" and "interim budget"?

  1. The provision of a "vote-on-account" is used by a regular Government, while an "interim budget" is a provision used by a caretaker Government.
  2. A "vote-on-account" only deals with the expenditure in the Government's budget, while an "interim budget" includes both expenditure and receipts.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Vote-on-Account vs. Interim Budget

Both are temporary measures, but they differ in scope.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: Both regular and caretaker governments can use either provision, though an interim budget is common in an election year.
  • Statement 2 is correct: A Vote-on-Account is strictly a grant in advance to cover expenditure for a short period (usually 2 months) until the full budget is passed. An Interim Budget is a complete set of accounts including both expenditure and receipts (revenue).

questions.question: Which one of the following is responsible for the preparation and presentation of the Union Budget to the Parliament?
questions.solution:
Preparation of the Union Budget

The responsibility for framing the budget lies within the Ministry of Finance.

  • The Department of Economic Affairs (specifically the Budget Division) is the nodal body responsible for preparing and presenting the Union Budget to the Parliament.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements: The Constitution of India provides that

  1. the Legislative Assembly of each State shall consist of not more than 450 members chosen by direct election from territorial constituencies in the State.
  2. a person shall not be qualified to be chosen to fill a seat in the Legislative Assembly of a State if he/she is less than 25 years of age.

Which of the statements above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
State Legislative Assembly Qualifications

The composition and qualifications for the Vidhan Sabha are specified in Articles 170 and 173.

  • Statement 1 is incorrect: Article 170 states the Assembly shall consist of not more than 500 members (not 450) and not less than 60 members.
  • Statement 2 is correct: Article 173(b) specifies that a person must be not less than 25 years of age to be eligible for a seat in the Legislative Assembly.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Rajya Sabha alone has the power to declare that it would be in national interest for the Parliament to legislate with respect to a matter in the State List.
  2. Resolutions approving the Proclamation of Emergency are passed only by the Lok Sabha.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Power over State List and Emergency

This contrasts the exclusive powers of the two Houses.

  • Statement 1 is correct: As per Article 249, the Rajya Sabha has the exclusive power to initiate the process of allowing Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in the national interest.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect: A proclamation of Emergency (Article 352) must be approved by both Houses of Parliament within one month. If the Lok Sabha is dissolved, the Rajya Sabha approves it, and the new Lok Sabha must approve it within 30 days of its first sitting.

questions.question: With reference to the Indian Parliament, which one of the following is not correct?
questions.option_1: The Appropriation Bill must be passed by both the Houses of Parliament before it can be enacted into law.
questions.option_2: No money shall be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund of India except under the appropriation made by the Appropriation Act.
questions.option_3: Finance Bill is required for proposing new taxes but no another Bill/Act is required for making changes in the rates of taxes which are already under operation.
questions.option_4: No Money Bill can be introduced except on the recommendation of the President.
questions.solution:
Constitutional Rules on Finance and Money Bills

The rules for financial legislation are strict to ensure the 'power of the purse' remains with the people's representatives.

  • Option 3 is the incorrect one: A Finance Bill is not just for 'new' taxes. Any bill that seeks to change the rates of existing taxes or continue them must also be passed as a Finance Bill.
  • Options 1, 2, and 4 are correct constitutional procedures under Articles 110, 114, and 117.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.option_1: In Lok Sabha, a no-confidence motion has to set out the grounds on which it is based.
questions.option_2: In the case of a no-confidence motion in Lok Sabha, no conditions of admissibility have been laid down in the Rules.
questions.option_3: A motion of no-confidence once admitted, has to be taken up within ten days of the leave being granted.
questions.option_4: Rajya Sabha is not empowered to entertain a motion of no-confidence.
questions.solution:
No-Confidence Motion in Lok Sabha

The No-Confidence Motion is a powerful tool to test the collective responsibility of the cabinet.

  • Option 1 is incorrect: According to the Rules of Procedure of Lok Sabha (Rule 198), a No-Confidence Motion does not need to set out specific grounds. It simply states that 'this House expresses its want of confidence in the Council of Ministers'.
  • Other points: It can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha. Once admitted (with 50 members' support), it must be discussed within 10 days.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Speaker of Lok Sabha has the power to adjourn the House sine die but, on prorogation, it is only the President who can summon the House.
  2. Unless sooner dissolved or there is an extension of the term, there is an automatic dissolution of the Lok Sabha by efflux of time, at the end of the period of five years, even if no formal order of dissolution is issued by the President.
  3. The Speaker of Lok Sabha continues in office even after the dissolution of the House and until immediately before the first meeting of the House.

Which of these statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Life of the Lok Sabha and the Speaker

The continuity of the Lok Sabha and its officers follows specific rules.

  • Statement 1 is correct: The Speaker adjourns the House sine die (without a day), but the President prorogues and later summons the House.
  • Statement 2 is correct: Under Article 83, the Lok Sabha has a term of 5 years. At the end of this period, it undergoes automatic dissolution by efflux of time.
  • Statement 3 is correct: The Speaker remains in office until immediately before the first meeting of the new Lok Sabha to ensure continuity in the presiding office.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. While members of the Rajya Sabha are associated with Committees on Public Accounts and Public Undertakings, members of Committee on Estimates are drawn entirely from Lok Sabha.
  2. The Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs works under the overall direction of Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs.
  3. The Minister of Parliamentary Affairs nominates Members of Parliament on Committees, Councils, Boards and Commissions etc. set up by the Government of India in the various ministries.

Which of these statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Parliamentary Committees and Ministry Affairs

Parliamentary committees are vital for detailed scrutiny of governance.

  • Statement 1 is correct: The Estimates Committee is unique because its members are only from the Lok Sabha. The PAC and CoPU include members from both Houses.
  • Statement 2 is correct: The Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs handles the scheduling of government business and follows the directions of the Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs.
  • Statement 3 is correct: The Minister of Parliamentary Affairs acts as the coordinator for nominating MPs to various statutory and non-statutory bodies set up by the Government.
